Population Ethics via Preference Maps

Formalising graphical representations of distribution-insensitive worlds using indifference curves

2026-06-28

0. Background

A philosophy student noticed that the standard graphical representation of the Repugnant Conclusion is misleading: shading rectangles under a curve is just a visual trick for multiplying two numbers — it is not actually representing worlds. He proposed instead that:

  • The x-axis = number of people \(N\)
  • The y-axis = average happiness \(\bar{w}\)
  • Each point \((N, \bar{w})\) represents a possible world

He then drew a downward-sloping straight line, arguing that anything above it is morally better than anything below it.

This document formalises that intuition using the economic tools of indifference curves and utility maximisation.

Why His Graph Is an Improvement

The standard illustration of the Repugnant Conclusion computes:

\[V = N \times \bar{w}\]

That is, it shades and compares rectangles. But notice:

The graph itself isn’t representing worlds. The rectangles are merely a graphical trick for multiplying two numbers.

Your friend’s insight was to make each point on the graph represent a possible society — a combination \((N, \bar{w})\). This is precisely how economists represent preferences over bundles of goods.

The Economic Interpretation

Suppose the moral evaluator has preferences over societies \((N, \bar{w})\). Instead of saying “value equals area,” we simply say there exists some social welfare function:

\[W(N, \bar{w})\]

that ranks societies. Every level set

\[W(N, \bar{w}) = c\]

is then an indifference curve — exactly as in consumer theory.

Indifference Curves for Different Ethical Theories

Total Utilitarianism

\[W = N \cdot \bar{w}\]

The level sets are:

\[N \cdot \bar{w} = c \implies \bar{w} = \frac{c}{N}\]

These are downward-sloping hyperbolas — familiar to any economist as standard indifference curves. They tell you exactly how much average welfare you are willing to sacrifice in exchange for additional people.

Average Utilitarianism

\[W = \bar{w}\]

The level sets are horizontal lines. Population size is entirely irrelevant to moral value.

Critical-Level Utilitarianism

\[W = N(\bar{w} - c)\]

where \(c > 0\) is the critical welfare level. The level sets are again hyperbolas, but shifted — a life is only worth adding if welfare exceeds the threshold \(c\).

Lexical and Rights-Based Views

These generate kinked or L-shaped indifference curves, possibly with vertical sections or asymptotes — but economists already know how to interpret such geometries.

What Your Friend Drew

A downward-sloping straight line is a single linear indifference curve:

\[\bar{w} = a - bN \quad (a, b > 0)\]

This implicitly says:

  • There is a trade-off between quantity and quality of life.
  • The trade-off is constant: each additional person always costs the same amount of average welfare.

Once indifference curves are introduced, he will realise there are infinitely many such curves — one for each level of moral value. The entire ethical theory is just the family of curves. This gives philosophers a much richer language:

Curve shape Ethical implication
Hyperbolas Total utilitarianism
Straight lines Constant trade-off (what your friend drew)
Convex curves Diminishing marginal moral value of additional people
Kinked curves Threshold views
Vertical asymptotes Rights-based theories
Non-convex curves Multiple moral regimes

The Marginal Rate of Substitution

This is where economics becomes especially powerful. The slope of an indifference curve is:

\[\text{MRS} = -\frac{\partial W / \partial N}{\partial W / \partial \bar{w}}\]

Translated into philosophy, this answers:

How much average welfare would we be willing to sacrifice in order to create one additional person while remaining morally indifferent?

That is an incredibly natural philosophical question. Instead of vague talk about “trade-offs,” we get a precise local concept. It also clarifies where disagreement between ethical views actually lies:

  • One theorist thinks additional people become less valuable as population grows → MRS falls in magnitude as \(N\) increases (convex curves).
  • Another thinks additional people remain equally valuable → constant MRS (straight lines).
  • Another insists there is a minimum acceptable welfare level → critical-level utilitarianism.

All of these become different geometries.

What Economics Adds

Economics separates two things that population ethicists often conflate:

  1. The representation of preferences — the shape of indifference curves.
  2. The axioms generating them — completeness, transitivity, continuity, and so on.

Population ethicists often jump straight to candidate social welfare functions (Total, Average, Critical-Level) without first exploiting this geometric framework. The indifference-curve approach reframes the question from:

Which formula computes value?

to:

What shape should the indifference curves have?

That is a much deeper question.

One Important Caveat

A point \((N, \bar{w})\) does not uniquely identify a world. Two societies could have identical population and identical average welfare but very different distributions of welfare — for example:

  • Everyone equally happy, or
  • Half ecstatic and half miserable.

This diagram implicitly assumes that only average welfare and population size matter. That is consistent with many discussions of the Repugnant Conclusion, but it abstracts away from inequality, prioritarianism, and other distribution-sensitive views. Including those would require additional dimensions or a richer state space.

Summary: A Suggested Order of Exposition

For a philosopher who is new to economics but already thinking graphically, the ideas can be introduced in this order:

  1. A society is a point \((N, \bar{w})\).
  2. A moral theory is a preference ordering over those points.
  3. Indifference curves represent equally good societies.
  4. Different ethical theories correspond to different families of curves.
  5. The slope of a curve gives the local trade-off between additional people and higher average welfare (the MRS).

This progression is likely to feel very natural to someone already thinking graphically about population ethics, and it provides a common language in which economists and philosophers can productively engage.

1. Preferences Maps

Worlds as bundles

In standard consumer theory, the object of choice is a consumption bundle \((x_1, x_2)\). Here the object is a world:

\[w = (N,\, \bar{u}), \quad N > 0,\; \bar{u} > 0\]

where \(N\) is population size (treated as continuous) and \(\bar{u}\) is average welfare. Every point in the positive quadrant of the \((N, \bar{u})\) plane is a possible world.

Scope limitation. This representation discards distributional information: two worlds with the same \((N, \bar{u})\) but different welfare distributions are treated as identical. The framework below applies only to distribution-insensitive ethical theories.

Each dot is a world (N, ū). We want to rank them.

Preference relations

Given two worlds \(w = (N, \bar{u})\) and \(w' = (N', \bar{u}')\), a moral agent holds one of three relations:

Relation Symbol Meaning
Strict preference \(w \succ w'\) \(w\) is strictly better than \(w'\)
Indifference \(w \sim w'\) \(w\) and \(w'\) are equally good
Weak preference \(w \succsim w'\) \(w\) is at least as good as \(w'\)

These are linked: \(w \succsim w'\) and \(w' \succsim w\) together imply \(w \sim w'\); \(w \succsim w'\) but not \(w' \succsim w\) implies \(w \succ w'\).

Preference as revealed through choice behaviour

In consumer theory, preferences are operational: \((x_1, x_2) \succ (y_1, y_2)\) means the consumer chooses \((x_1, x_2)\) when both are available.

In population ethics the analogue is hypothetical choice: \(w \succ w'\) means you would choose to bring world \(w\) into existence rather than \(w'\). Preferences can be elicited through policy deliberation, willingness-to-fund studies, or philosophical thought experiments.

2 Assumptions about Preferences

Why axioms are imposed

Without restrictions, \(\succsim\) can be arbitrary — cyclical, incomplete, or discontinuous. Axioms are normative hypotheses about rational ethical judgment. Some population-ethics intuitions violate them (e.g. Parfit’s Mere Addition Paradox troubles transitivity).

Completeness

For any \(w, w'\): \(\;w \succsim w'\) or \(w' \succsim w\) (or both).

Every pair of worlds is comparable. Ethical price: you cannot declare two worlds incommensurable — e.g. refuse to rank “10 billion people at barely positive welfare” against “1 million at very high welfare.”

Reflexivity

\(w \succsim w\) for all \(w\). Trivial: every world is at least as good as itself.

Transitivity

If \(w \succsim w'\) and \(w' \succsim w''\), then \(w \succsim w''\). No preference cycles.

Ethical price: some sequences of pairwise reasonable judgments lead to cycles in population ethics. Transitivity rules these out by fiat.

Existence of a “best” world

Transitivity is necessary for a most-preferred world to exist in any finite set. Without it, \(w_A \succ w_B \succ w_C \succ w_A\) gives a cycle — no world is best. Completeness + transitivity together guarantee a best element in every finite set.

3 Indifference Curves

Weakly preferred sets

Fix a world \(w_0 = (N_0, \bar{u}_0)\). The weakly preferred set is:

\[P(w_0) = \{(N, \bar{u}) : (N, \bar{u}) \succsim w_0\}\]

Its boundary — worlds equally good as \(w_0\) — is the indifference curve through \(w_0\).

Shaded: weakly preferred set. Boundary = indifference curve through w₀.

Construction of indifference curves

Start at \((N_0, \bar{u}_0)\). Increase \(N\) by \(\Delta N\); ask how much \(\bar{u}\) must decrease to restore indifference. Repeat to trace the curve. The answer at each step is the MRS (§6.).

Why indifference curves cannot cross

Claim: Two distinct indifference curves cannot intersect.

Proof. Suppose curves \(C_1 \neq C_2\) cross at \(Z\). Take \(X \in C_1 \setminus C_2\) and \(Y \in C_2 \setminus C_1\).

  • \(X, Z\) both on \(C_1\): \(X \sim Z\)
  • \(Y, Z\) both on \(C_2\): \(Y \sim Z\)
  • Transitivity: \(X \sim Y\)

But \(C_1 \neq C_2\) means \(X\) and \(Y\) represent different welfare levels — contradiction. \(\square\)

If the curves crossed at Z then X ~ Z ~ Y implies X ~ Y — contradicting distinct welfare levels.

Relationship between preferences and the indifference map

The indifference map — the full collection of curves — partitions the \((N, \bar{u})\) plane. Every world lies on exactly one curve. Moving to a higher curve (under monotonicity) means a strictly preferred world.

4. Examples of Preferences

These examples show how different ethical dispositions imply different curve shapes, all consistent with §1.2 axioms.

Perfect Substitutes

\[W(N, \bar{u}) = aN + b\bar{u}\]

You are always willing to exchange \(a/b\) units of \(\bar{u}\) for one extra person at a constant rate, regardless of current levels. Indifference curves are parallel straight lines with slope \(-a/b\).

Perfect substitutes (W = N + 2ū): constant trade-off. Parallel lines, slope = −1/2.

Ethical content. The marginal value of an extra person (in welfare units) is constant — independent of how many people exist or how well-off they are.

Perfect Complements

\[W(N, \bar{u}) = \min\!\left\{\frac{N}{a},\, \frac{\bar{u}}{b}\right\}\]

Population and welfare are only valuable together, in fixed proportion \(a:b\). Extra population without proportional welfare improvement adds nothing (and vice versa). Indifference curves are L-shaped, with kinks along the ray \(\bar{u} = (b/a)N\).

Perfect complements (W = min{N, ū}): L-shaped curves. Kinks along the diagonal ū = N.

Ethical content. More people is worthless without proportional welfare (and vice versa). Scale and quality are inseparable requirements of a valuable world.

Bads

A bad is a quantity the agent dislikes — more is worse. In population ethics this can arise when the axes are reinterpreted:

  • \(N\) = number of deaths: more deaths is bad. To stay indifferent while deaths increase, welfare must rise to compensate. Indifference curves slope upward (positive slope).
  • \(\bar{u}\) = average suffering: more suffering is bad.

The main body of §1.1–§1.6 treats \(N\) and \(\bar{u}\) as goods. §7. covers the bads case.

N is a bad (deaths). More deaths require higher welfare to compensate. Curves slope upward; preferred direction is north-west.

Vertical indifference curves

A dimension is neutral when the agent is completely indifferent to changes in it. Two cases arise in population ethics:

  • \(N\) is neutral (only \(\bar{u}\) matters): worlds with the same \(\bar{u}\) are equally good regardless of \(N\). Indifference curves are horizontal lines. This is average utilitarianism (\(W = \bar{u}\)).
  • \(\bar{u}\) is neutral (only \(N\) matters): worlds with the same \(N\) are equally good regardless of \(\bar{u}\). Indifference curves are vertical lines. This is pure headcount (\(W = N\)).

Left: N is neutral (average utilitarianism, W = ū) — horizontal curves. Right: ū is neutral (pure headcount, W = N) — vertical curves.

Satiation

There exists an ideal world \((N^*, \bar{u}^*)\) — a bliss point. Worlds further from it are worse. Beyond the bliss point, \(N\) and \(\bar{u}\) each become bads. Indifference curves are closed ovals around the bliss point; inner ovals are strictly preferred to outer ones.

Satiation: bliss point (red dot). Indifference curves are concentric ellipses. Inner oval = better.

Beyond the bliss point, indifference curves acquire a positive slope (in the regions where only one dimension has been “satiated”) and curve back to form closed ovals.

5. Well-Behaved Preferences

We now impose two axioms that characterise the standard case where \(N\) and \(\bar{u}\) are both genuine goods.

Monotonicity

Definition. If \(N' \geq N\) and \(\bar{u}' \geq \bar{u}\) with at least one strict inequality, then \((N', \bar{u}') \succ (N, \bar{u})\).

Ethical content. Both population size and average welfare are genuine goods — more of either strictly improves the world. Monotonicity rules out satiation and the view that \(N\) is ethically neutral.

Implication. Monotonicity \(\Rightarrow\) indifference curves have negative slope. Moving north-east from any point reaches a strictly better world; to stay equally good when moving right, you must move down.

Monotonic preferences: direction of preference is north-east. Curves slope downward.

Convexity

Definition. For any \(w \sim w'\), the blend \(w_t = tw + (1-t)w'\) satisfies \(w_t \succsim w\) for all \(t \in [0,1]\).

Ethical content. Balanced worlds are preferred to extremes. Given two equally-good worlds — one with large \(N\) and low \(\bar{u}\), another with small \(N\) and high \(\bar{u}\) — any blend is at least as good.

Implication. The weakly preferred set \(P(w_0)\) is convex; indifference curves are bowed inward toward the origin. Any chord connecting two equally-good worlds lies above the curve.

Strict convexity requires \(w_t \succ w\) for \(t \in (0,1)\) — no flat segments. This guarantees a unique most-preferred world when a constraint is imposed.

Three preference geometries. Convex (left): averages preferred to extremes, curves bow inward. Concave (right): extremes preferred.

6. The Marginal Rate of Substitution (MRS)

Definition and slope of the indifference curve

The MRS at \((N, \bar{u})\) is the absolute slope of the indifference curve at that point:

\[\text{MRS}(N, \bar{u}) \;=\; -\frac{d\bar{u}}{dN}\bigg|_{W = \text{const}} \;=\; \frac{\partial W/\partial N}{\partial W/\partial \bar{u}} \;>\; 0\]

It is the amount of average welfare you are willing to give up in exchange for one additional person while remaining on the same indifference curve.

MRS = |slope| of the indifference curve at (N₀, ū₀). Tangent line shown dashed.

Marginal substitution interpretation

MRS large (steep curve) MRS small (flat curve)
Large \(\bar{u}\) sacrifice to accept one more person Small \(\bar{u}\) sacrifice per extra person
High weight on welfare quality vs. population High weight on population vs. welfare quality
Typical when \(N\) is already large Typical when \(N\) is small

Under convex preferences, MRS decreases as \(N\) increases along a curve — diminishing MRS. The more people there already are, the less you value one more at the margin.

Exchange interpretation and tangency condition

Suppose population can be “traded” for welfare at a fixed exchange rate \(E\): gaining \(\Delta N\) extra people costs \(E \cdot \Delta N\) units of \(\bar{u}\). The achievable trades from \((N_0, \bar{u}_0)\) lie on a line through \((N_0, \bar{u}_0)\) with slope \(-E\).

  • If \(E < \text{MRS}\): trading at rate \(E\) is a good deal — moves to a better indifference curve.
  • If \(E > \text{MRS}\): trading is too costly — moves to a worse curve.
  • If \(E = \text{MRS}\): tangency condition — the exchange line just touches the curve. This is the locally optimal point; no trade improves welfare.

Exchange line with slope −E tangent to the indifference curve. At the tangency point, E = MRS — no trade improves welfare.

7. Special case: the Repugnant Conclusion

Parfit’s Repugnant Conclusion (RC): under total utilitarianism, for any world \(A\) with large \(N\) and high \(\bar{u}\), there exists a world \(Z\) with a vastly larger \(N\) and \(\bar{u}\) barely above zero that is ethically better.

Total utilitarianism

\[W(N, \bar{u}) = N \cdot \bar{u}, \quad \text{MRS} = \frac{\bar{u}}{N}\]

Indifference curves are rectangular hyperbolae: \(\bar{u} = c/N\). As \(N \to \infty\), MRS \(\to 0\): you need only an infinitesimal \(\bar{u}\) drop to accept one more person. The hyperbolae sweep arbitrarily close to \(\bar{u} = 0\), making the RC geometrically inevitable.

Total utilitarianism. Z (huge pop, tiny welfare) lies on a higher curve than A. The RC is unavoidable.

The utility monster: the same problem in reverse

Nozick’s utility monster (1974) is a being that converts resources into welfare far more efficiently than ordinary people. Under total utilitarianism, you should give it everything.

In the original form, this is a distribution problem within a fixed population, and the \((N, \bar{u})\) framework hides it: since \(\bar{u}\) is already an average, a world where one person has welfare \(10^6\) and everyone else has welfare \(0\) looks identical to a world with even distribution, provided the mean is the same.

There is, however, a population-ethics analog that is directly representable — should we create one super-happy being rather than many ordinarily happy people? Under \(W = N\bar{u}\):

\[W_M = 1 \times 2000 = 2000 \quad > \quad W_B = 100 \times 10 = 1000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad M \succ B\]

The monster-world \(M = (1, 2000)\) beats the balanced world \(B = (100, 10)\) because it lies on a higher hyperbola.

This is geometrically symmetric with the RC:

Problem Direction on plane Mechanism
Repugnant Conclusion Lower-right (more \(N\), less \(\bar{u}\)) Higher hyperbola reachable with huge \(N\) and tiny \(\bar{u}\)
Utility monster Upper-left (less \(N\), more \(\bar{u}\)) Higher hyperbola reachable with \(N=1\) and enormous \(\bar{u}\)

Both are possible for the same reason: rectangular hyperbolae extend without bound in both directions. Unlimited substitution between \(N\) and \(\bar{u}\) is always available.

RC and utility monster are mirror images. A (reference) sits on W = 4000. Both Z (RC direction) and M (monster direction) sit on the higher curve W = 8000 — and are equally good as each other under total utilitarianism.

What indifference maps avoid the RC?

To avoid the RC, no indifference curve may dip arbitrarily close to \(\bar{u} = 0\).

Average utilitarianism: \(W = \bar{u}\) — horizontal lines. Avoids RC completely; \(N\) is neutral (violates monotonicity in \(N\)).

Average utilitarianism: horizontal IC. Population size carries zero weight.

Critical-level utilitarianism: \(W = N(\bar{u} - \alpha)\), \(\alpha > 0\). Adding a person is good only if their welfare exceeds \(\alpha\). Indifference curves are shifted hyperbolae with horizontal asymptote at \(\bar{u} = \alpha\). No curve dips below \(\alpha\), so the RC is avoided.

\[\bar{u} = \alpha + \frac{c}{N}\]

Critical-level utilitarianism (α = 10). Curves cannot dip below ū = α. RC avoided.

8.When \(N\) or \(\bar{u}\) are bads

The framework accommodates reinterpretations of the axes.

\(N\) = number of deaths: preferred direction is north-west; curves slope upward. More deaths requires higher welfare to compensate.

Both are bads (e.g. \(N\) = deaths, \(\bar{u}\) = average disutility): preferred direction is south-west; curves slope downward again but preference reverses.

Left: N is a bad (deaths). Right: both N and ū are bads. Preferred direction shown by arrow.

9. Summary tables

Axioms → curve properties

Axiom Statement Ethical price Curve property
Completeness Any \(w, w'\) are comparable No incommensurable pairs allowed Curves partition the plane
Reflexivity \(w \succsim w\) Trivial Every world lies on its own curve
Transitivity No cycles Spectrum arguments may violate it Distinct curves cannot cross
Continuity No ethical cliffs Rules out lexicographic priorities Curves are smooth
Monotonicity More \(N\) and \(\bar{u}\) both better Excludes satiation; \(N\) must be a good Curves have negative slope
Convexity Averages preferred to extremes Balance is a substantive assumption Curves bow inward; MRS decreasing

Ethical theories as special cases

Theory \(W(N, \bar{u})\) Curve shape RC? Monotone in \(N\)?
Total utilitarianism \(N\bar{u}\) Rectangular hyperbolae Yes Yes
Average utilitarianism \(\bar{u}\) Horizontal lines No No
Critical-level (\(\alpha > 0\)) \(N(\bar{u} - \alpha)\) Shifted hyperbolae No Yes (above \(\alpha\))
Perfect substitutes \(aN + b\bar{u}\) Parallel lines Depends on \(a, b\) Yes
Perfect complements \(\min\{N/a, \bar{u}/b\}\) L-shaped Depends Yes
Satiation — Concentric ovals No No (beyond bliss)

Examples of preferences: curve-shape summary

Preference type \(W\) example Curve shape MRS
Perfect substitutes \(N + 2\bar{u}\) Parallel straight lines Constant
Perfect complements \(\min\{N, \bar{u}\}\) L-shaped (kinks on diagonal) 0 or \(\infty\)
Bads (\(N\) = deaths) \(\bar{u} - 0.25N\) Upward-sloping Negative (positive slope)
\(N\) neutral \(\bar{u}\) Horizontal lines \(0\)
\(\bar{u}\) neutral \(N\) Vertical lines \(\infty\)
Satiation — Closed ovals around \((N^*, \bar{u}^*)\) Changes sign beyond bliss
Well-behaved \(N\bar{u}\) Convex hyperbolae \(\bar{u}/N\) (decreasing in \(N\))

Compiled with R 4.5.2. Plots: ggplot2, gridExtra.
